items.q: How do you say 'I play the piano' vs 'I play football'?
items.correct: Je joue DU piano (instrument) vs je joue AU football (sport).
items.misconception: Same preposition for both - 'je joue piano', 'je joue football'.
items.random_wrong: Je joue le piano, je joue le football.
items.why: Students drop or confuse prepositions. The rule: jouer DE + instrument (de, du, de la); jouer À + sport/game (à, au, aux). Easy way to lose marks in speaking exams about hobbies.

items.q: How do you say 'I am using my phone right now' (emphasising the action is in progress)?
items.correct: Je suis en train d'utiliser mon portable.
items.why: Students translate English '-ing' as a participle. French has no progressive tense - the present 'j'utilise' covers both 'I use' and 'I am using'. For emphasis on the ongoing action, use 'être en train de + infinitive'.

items.q: How do you say 'I like coffee' in French?
items.why: English drops articles for general likes/dislikes; French keeps them. For general preferences, use the DEFINITE article: j'aime le chocolat, je déteste les épinards.
Correct answer: cependant. “donc” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which time phrase signals the FUTURE tense?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: la semaine prochaine. “hier” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many connectives are in this sentence: 'J'aime le sport et la musique, mais je n'aime pas la lecture parce que c'est ennuyeux'?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
